speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four, an interview with Dr. Andreas Chrambach. You were talking about the barbed wire. There was-- you had some kind of communication?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I was just saying that I was summoned for that offense. And it was a sort of offense for which I knew, exactly, people paid with their lives in the camp. But somehow, I attributed it again, like at the time of my interrogation in Budapest, to my military manners, my perfect German. This guy was Ukrainian because they were-- the SS were mostly non-German speaking Ukrainians and Yugoslavs in the camps. Obviously, people couldn't be trusted to fight. So-- so they employed them in these jobs. Well, anyway, this guy must have been impressed by my youth, by my innocent looks, by my Prussian manners, whatnot. So after ripping off a few of-- my-- my number, and a few of the buttons that were in the wrong place, I was given the sign to-- to leave. And I was-- ran off happy to have my life saved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your brother all this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK. It must have been after this incidence that I developed a viral disease-- red pimples that spread over the entire leg. And people were hysterical about infectious diseases in the camp. So I was placed instantly into the hospital block, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] where I was not allowed to get out. And-- and my brother, in the evening, after coming back from the work detail-- he always came to the-- the window in the block to find me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, that-- one day he didn't anymore. And apparently what had happened is they had taken him to Sachsenhausen, which was the concentration camp closest to Berlin. I know this only from later from documentation because he was registered there as a Hungarian interpreter. And the same thing once more in Buchenwald, when he perished. Must have been very close to the end of the war. So, from then on, I was separated from my brother. OK. [SIGHS] Otherwise, all right, now this-- this-- this infection proved to be a godsend because, first of all, it kept me inside when it was extremely cold on the outside. [? I mean, ?] these work details had to work at minus 30, minus 35 degrees Centigrade. Up in Silesia, it gets very cold and snow. And I was lying on my straw on a blanket, being visited by the-- by the physician of the block-- physicians of the block. Now, it turns out that these physicians-- one of these physicians happened to be a neighbor of my grandmother's in Berlin, also an [? upper-classy ?] German who knew my grandmother and, obviously, also realized I was a kid and whatnot. And-- and-- and both he and the-- Paul Self, the guy who beat up the Portuguese and a number of other Frenchmen in his-- in his surroundings-- also adopted me because I spoke fluent French. That had to do for-- with the-- with the Belgian history. So-- so-- so they made me into a male nurse, where my job was to wash operation-- to wash blood and pus off operation tables. And-- and not a-- not really a-- a fun job. But, again, it was warm. Moreover, it gave me two spoons full of soup in the evening, rather than what ordinary citizens got, which was one spoonful or one ladle spoon, one ladle full of soup. Got two ladles. Well, that obviously prolonged your life. So-- so being a male nurse there, obviously, was a-- was a-- was a-- was-- was one of the elements of my survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: The physicians, were they Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, all of them. Everybody was Jewish. Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it a serious-minded hospital setting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, strangely enough-- strangely enough, it was. I mean, these physicians got terrible cases in. You know, for instance, out of the salt-- out of the coal mines that were a part of the camp, they got patients who had been hit by the foreman with a pickaxes. And they had wounds that-- that deep, that size, just filled with pus, not treated, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these physicians tried to save these guys. Of course, most of the time, couldn't. But they-- they did what they could, which was very little. Very little, obviously, very few tools, practically no-- no-- no-- no medicines.

speaker: interviewee
text: They worked-- they worked themselves to death to-- to help others. There's no question about it. They were very, very decent. I don't remember many of the names of the Frenchmen. The name of [NON-ENGLISH], I remember, was a-- he was a surgeon. There was this guy from Berlin. There was Self and a number of others.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you ill before you--

speaker: interviewee
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: --before you became a male nurse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, maybe-- maybe couple of weeks, not more than that. You see, because [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] maybe-- well, maybe more than that. Maybe four weeks. Maybe four weeks, something like this. OK, now then, really only-- only one other [? scene ?] from the-- from Birkenau, which was Auschwitz II, where I was. I didn't mention that. The-- the large camp in Auschwitz was Auschwitz II. You will-- you will be interested to know when I found out that this was an extermination camp. And I was still when I was together with my brother. Must have been early first-- first few evenings after work. We were standing by close, overlooking, again, the-- the barbed wire that led into the women's camp and then, beyond that, the gypsies camp. And there were about 20 of these lined up, one against the other. I have no idea what went on in the others.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were standing there, us, and-- and-- and there were these fires in the sky, these flames-- giant flames in the sky, as a building was burning. And so, in our innocence, we asked the next guy next-- standing next to us what [? in the hell ?] is burning? And he looked at us, big-eyed, and said, if we didn't know, he was not going to tell us because every day we didn't know was better for us. So it's really only bit by bit we found out what was going on. And you could see that from our-- from our rectangular-- [? yeah, ?] I forget the name of these. The block was the house. But I don't remember the name of the-- of the compound, the whole compound. I know it was Compound A.

speaker: interviewee
text: But standing there in the evening, we could certainly see the-- the fire every night for-- for a while. And, of course, these, [? now ?] having been back at Birkenau-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, I realize that next to this-- and, of course, also from reading-- I know that this was also the terminal of the-- of the railroad line inside of the camp, where people were unloaded from railroad trains and immediately gassed and burned, without ever, ever entering the-- the sort of Birkenau, the sort of Auschwitz camp that I was living in, which was, in fact, the work camp. The guard hadn't completely lied. And, in fact, you know, while I was disassembling airplanes and building roads and this sort of thing, other people, in fact, worked in textile factories and in the coal mines, as I mentioned already, a number of industrial outfits that, you know, where the mechanism was always set by the-- by truck full. You were shipped out in the morning to-- to your work, to your work detail. And that started at maybe 5:00 in the morning and went on till late at night. Very little sleep, and obviously, one ladle full of rather watery soup, maybe a piece of bread every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So OK, so under those circumstances, of course, most people lost weight all the time. And you could-- you could-- you could sort of extrapolate how long you could survive. I would say, on the-- in the camp that I knew, your chances of staying alive were not-- not more than about a year. If-- of course, if you're well-- well nourished, like we were, and muscular, was a little bit longer. If you are young, it was longer than if you are old, and so on. But there was a category in the camp that was called a Muselmann, a Muslim. A Muselmann was somebody who was now emaciated to such degree he couldn't work anymore, you see? And the institution-- the camp's institution of selection that I must have participated in, maybe still two or three times, maybe four times-- consisted, in essence, of-- of weeding out the Muselmann, the people who had emaciated to such a degree they couldn't work anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're not useful to the-- to the Reich anymore. A very [? rational ?] operation. At least that, I think, was the principle. In fact, they did, of course, send all of these guys to-- to death, once your-- your bones were sticking out, and you obviously couldn't work anymore. Well, I was never in that category, obviously, for obvious reasons because that time was long-- not long enough. And my previous conditions was too good. And then also, I had this absence of an A on my arm, which helped. And I also knew Prussian manners-- I mean, how to walk by the SS officers in a erect fashion and self-confident fashion. That-- that-- that played also a large role, you know, because all in theory, this institution existed to weed out Muselmanns. I am convinced that if you look by the criteria of the SS, particularly Jewish, your chances were much augmented of being gassed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- your daily ration, you-- you indicated a ladle of soup--

speaker: interviewee
text: We got a ladle of soup in the evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or two, in my case, when I was a nurse, right. That was sort of a cabbage-- cabbage soup [? mess. ?] There was always a great amusement when people found a-- a-- a fiber of meat in it. I mean, it was great exhilaration if they discovered a fiber of meat in it. I-- only on rarest of occasions. Well, it was-- it was clearly, you know, I don't know how many calories. But the-- the operation of the work camp, to my mind, clearly had the purpose of utilizing the muscle power of the people involved to the-- for the benefit of the-- of Germany and its industry and as long as possible. I mean, you put-- you put 1,000 calories a day in, and you take 4,000 out. And under those circumstances, you know, you could plot if you had-- had graph paper, you could have plotted how long you were going to stay, which, I think-- I think that's-- it's about a year. And-- and I'm extremely suspicious of people who was-- who were in concentration camps for longer periods than that, at least the concentration camps that I knew, because it was impossible, unless you-- you had-- you had acquired rank. And the only way you could acquire rank was to-- was to be a traitor on your fellow prisoners, you know, which was very obvious. Every night-- you see, every night, the-- the evening ended with what-- an institution that's called the appell. The appell meant that everybody had to line up in-- in rows of 10 and be counted. And the official purpose of this thing was to make sure nobody escaped. So-- so the lead-- the kapos and the Lageralteste-- the-- the chief of the-- of the-- of the compound-- stood in front of you, like-- like the officers in the army and counted you. Gave a report-- 693 men here. And actually, we had to stand at attention, with our hats off. And 46 in the sick. And that adds up to whatever. And so people could do their counting. See, but these nightly occasions. It was-- became, of course, very obvious what the rank differences were. And so, I remember some of the-- in particular, two chieftains of the compound, one a German communist and the other a Russian general, you know, who moved [? with us, ?] they were absolutely safe. Nothing-- you know, they could have survived concentration camp for 10 years, which obviously, the communists did. Well, they obviously had locked them up at the latest, after the Spanish civil war in '36. So he had been there for eight years, and nothing to worry about. And to make it worse, you have these guys in front of the other prisoners, showing off their wealth, I remember. There were SS people actually present in the nightly appell because the-- the-- the number of people involved were-- again, at attention-- were communicated to the governing SS people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: But not only this, but the-- the-- the communist prisoner nightly pulled out a golden-- golden cigarette case and offered the SS his cigarettes. You know, these guys had everything. They had servants. They had children working for them. You know, people felt-- that may or may not be the case they were all pedophiles. I-- I-- I doubt that they're all pedophiles, but probably some of them. But-- but-- but part of rank in the concentration-- in Auschwitz consisted of having servants, including children, who polished your shoes. And, obviously, these guys had exquisitely ironed pants. I think, you know, so one thing that stands out in my mind about that time is the-- the uncontrollable hatred that I felt for these people who had taken advantage of their fellow prisoners by saving their own skins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall or observed any attempts at suicide or actual suicides?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Not at all. Not at all. You remember, I was kneeling every night and praying to my god. I mean, that-- I was ready for death. There was no question about it. Everybody was. You know, the-- the-- the chances of escape were nil. And nobody believed that you would ever be free again-- nobody. And when you were religious, as I was, you simply came to terms with death, in terms of your religion, that said you were going to. And it-- I-- I'm sure it-- it-- it did happen. But there was no thought of ever coming out alive. So-- so that-- that continued, my work as a male nurse, until, I would say, the middle of January 1945. At that time, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] anybody who could walk had already been shipped off. They were being [? stripped. ?] People were called out. They formed these famous rows of 10-- you know, battalions in rows of 10, with guards on both sides. And they were marched off. And physicians and male nurses were finally marched off around the 15th of-- of January.

speaker: interviewer
text: Marched off from where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, marched off from where I was, which was Auschwitz II, Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- in the-- in the-- still in the hospital compound. I should say that the-- the fires and also the selections were discontinued in the middle of December. And, you know, from hindsight, I know now that actually the crematoria were blown up at that time. So that practice did-- was stopped because the Red Army, in fact, had-- had approached very close. In fact, maybe before I tell you about the march to Auschwitz-- one, I should tell you, that in the-- in the few days before that, I was drafted into a work detail that shipped furniture and personal belongings of SS people from their villas onto trains. And we-- we carried out the-- we were movers-- we were employed as movers. And one of the fortunate things that happened in that transaction, in that work detail, is that one of the SS officers threw me a pair of old boots that I wore for months afterwards. And they get a piece of luck, you see, because once your feet are frozen off, that was extremely important to have-- to have shoes that-- that would not leak-- the snow would not leak through. So I had these good boots. And, again, you know, why the hell he took pity on me, I don't know. But I suspect seeing an innocent 17-year-old who obviously had nothing to do with all of this-- couldn't even comprehend any of it-- he must have seen that somehow.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this entire period, did you have any knowledge of what was going on in terms of politics and--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the Americans, the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no, not at all. There was absolutely zero information. Nothing. And also no concern, you see, because once you've given up on your life, you-- you don't worry about anymore what goes on outside. There was actually no political-- even the political interest or discussion or anything like this. Everybody kept pretty much to-- for himself, largely-- as I mentioned already-- for fear of being turned in, if you said the wrong thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So OK. Then came the middle of January. And we were marched off. We [? simply ?] made, I would think, 200, 300 people-- mostly physicians and-- and-- and male nurses-- were marched off, leaving behind those sick people who-- who could not walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- and we were given a big block-- maybe a kilogram-- of margarine and a giant loaf of bread each. And we were-- and they told us this had to last us for a week. We should be careful with it. Well, then, of course, I was so starved, ate it immediately, the margarine and the bread, within minutes. And whether or not this had the effect or not, I don't know. But the fact is that we-- the first march led us to Auschwitz I, which is very different kind of camp. The original Auschwitz I, these are brick buildings. Again, I have pictures, if you want to revisit all of this. These are brick buildings with various stories. And my French comrades and I ended up, I think, on the top floor of one of these big brick buildings in Auschwitz I for the night. I think-- I think my friend Paul Self claimed that this was his bright idea. Maybe it was, but I would-- would rather think it was the margarine and the bread that made us oversleep the-- when the transport left off. They were whistling. And the SS was rather nervous already at that time because you could actually hear gunfire. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how long of a march was it from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that must've--

speaker: interviewer
text: --from II to I?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, must have been a day's march.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the fact was that when I woke up in the morning, on top of this building, the place was empty. Everybody had left. And that happens to be, also, a-- a-- a hospital compound in Auschwitz I, where we were.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was good news. And then I think we mingled with the-- whatever was left there. And-- and I don't think there were only sick people in Auschwitz I. There must have been still physicians around because there were people whom we didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so we woke up. We intermingled with them. And then, of course, I think by that afternoon, there was another call for-- for march. And actually, I lined up in it, being stupid. And-- but had the-- the-- the-- the instinctive wisdom to call in my famous Prussian manner to the next guard that I had to get in to get something. I don't know what it was. And these guards were really nervous. Close to nervous breakdown at that point because they know they're just around to be caught. So-- so he didn't shoot at me or do anything. I ran into the compound and slid into the next bed. And, in fact, while I was in the bed, that march went off. So that's how I was liberated, essentially. You know, there were a few tense days after that, where everybody claimed that the people who couldn't walk were going to be blown up. But we weren't blown up. At one point, they sent a guy out, like the pigeon in Noah's ark, to find out-- and meanwhile, there was a lot of shooting, of course, around-- whether-- whether it was safe to get out. Well, the chap never returned. So we stayed in our-- in our beds. I have no idea how we found nourishment until that time. I don't remember that. But anyway, after having been in the hospital bed there, with-- a-- in a-- in a-- in a hospital compound with other people who couldn't walk anymore-- a column of Red Army soldiers suddenly was-- came in to the camp, the main--

speaker: interviewer
text: What date--

speaker: interviewee
text: --street of Auschwitz I.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The main-- it was 28th of January, 1946-- well, '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: '45, 28th of January. So needless to say, those of us who could walk ran out and embraced the soldiers. And I said, [GERMAN]. Welcome. And the soldiers, strangely enough, were mostly Mongols, tiny Mongols, with sub-machine guns. So that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then OK, nothing really then happened, except that the Red Army moved an army hospital into the compound. We were put in the care of a Red Army captain, a female captain that took excellent care of us. Fed us. A lot of people died during that period because, obviously, they were not accustomed to eating, and they overfed them. But and there were also other things. I mean, I remember during that time, I-- I took care of several days of people had gone insane. There was a-- was a whole ward of people who had gone insane. And-- and I remember spending my days sort of stroking their heads, which was the only way to make them at all comfortable. [? And ?] [? amen. ?] And I-- I-- I was also in charge of heating the compound. I must've carried coal to 20 or 25 coal stoves every morning. And I remember polishing other people's shoes, and so I made myself useful. But I was still in the-- in the company of three or four or five Frenchmen from the Birkenau days who had somehow done-- and I have no idea how they had actually managed to stick around. But probably more wisely than I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall your emotional reaction to the-- to the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, very distinctly. I mean, it has never left me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Perhaps we should wait till the next tape--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to describe that. This is the end of tape number four.
